title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
code: var st = new Stack<int>();                 // increasing stack of indices
int cur = (i == n) ? 0 : heights[i];   // sentinel 0 flushes the stack
int width = i - left - 1;          // span strictly between boundaries

frames.caption: Increasing stack of indices, best = 0. Scan i = 0..n; index n is the sentinel 0.
frames.note: We keep a stack of bar INDICES (not heights) whose heights strictly increase from bottom to top. Storing indices lets each pop recover both its height (heights[idx]) and its position (for the width). The loop runs one past the end so a virtual height-0 bar can flush everything. O(n) time, O(n) space — every index is pushed once and popped once.

frames.caption: i = 0: cur = heights[0] = 2. Compare against the stack top before pushing.
frames.note: Each iteration first drains every stack bar that the current bar is too short to let continue (the while-loop), then pushes the current index. The current bar both ends taller bars’ rectangles and starts its own run.

frames.caption: Push index 0 (height 2). The stack stays increasing; bar 0 now waits for its own shorter bar.
frames.note: The current bar is at least as tall as the stack top, so the increasing run continues — this bar might still extend rightward into taller territory, so its rectangle is not yet decided. We push its index and move on. The stack always holds a strictly increasing sequence of heights (by index), which is exactly the invariant that lets each pop know its left boundary instantly.

frames.caption: i = 1: cur = heights[1] = 1. Compare against the stack top before pushing.
frames.note: Each iteration first drains every stack bar that the current bar is too short to let continue (the while-loop), then pushes the current index. The current bar both ends taller bars’ rectangles and starts its own run.

frames.caption: heights[top] = heights[0] = 2 ≥ cur = 1 → pop bar 0 and measure it.
frames.note: A shorter bar just arrived, so the bar on top can NEVER extend past it — this is the precise moment its maximal rectangle is finalized. The popped bar's height is the limiting height; the right boundary is the current index i (first shorter bar to the right) and the left boundary is the new stack top (first shorter bar to the left, or -1). Width = i - left - 1 spans every bar strictly between those two shorter walls. We score h*width against best.

frames.caption: left = -1 (bar below / -1), width = i - left - 1 = 1 - -1 - 1 = 1, area = 2×1 = 2.
frames.note: The subtle part: width is NOT i - poppedIndex. It is the full span strictly between the left boundary (the element now below on the stack, or -1 if empty) and i. That is why the formula reaches for the element BELOW the popped one — every bar between those two shorter walls was at least this tall, so the rectangle of this height stretches across all of them.

frames.caption: area 2 > best → best = 2. New largest rectangle: height 2 across width 1.
frames.note: best tracks the biggest finalized rectangle seen so far. Here height 2 × width 1 = 2 beats the previous best, so it becomes the running champion. The pair of bars 5,6 produces the winner (height 5 over width 2 = 10).

frames.caption: Push index 1 (height 1). The stack stays increasing; bar 1 now waits for its own shorter bar.
frames.note: The current bar is at least as tall as the stack top, so the increasing run continues — this bar might still extend rightward into taller territory, so its rectangle is not yet decided. We push its index and move on. The stack always holds a strictly increasing sequence of heights (by index), which is exactly the invariant that lets each pop know its left boundary instantly.

frames.caption: heights[top] = heights[3] = 6 ≥ cur = 2 → pop bar 3 and measure it.
frames.note: A shorter bar just arrived, so the bar on top can NEVER extend past it — this is the precise moment its maximal rectangle is finalized. The popped bar's height is the limiting height; the right boundary is the current index i (first shorter bar to the right) and the left boundary is the new stack top (first shorter bar to the left, or -1). Width = i - left - 1 spans every bar strictly between those two shorter walls. We score h*width against best.

frames.caption: left = 2 (bar below / -1), width = i - left - 1 = 4 - 2 - 1 = 1, area = 6×1 = 6.
frames.note: The subtle part: width is NOT i - poppedIndex. It is the full span strictly between the left boundary (the element now below on the stack, or -1 if empty) and i. That is why the formula reaches for the element BELOW the popped one — every bar between those two shorter walls was at least this tall, so the rectangle of this height stretches across all of them.

frames.caption: area 6 > best → best = 6. New largest rectangle: height 6 across width 1.
frames.note: best tracks the biggest finalized rectangle seen so far. Here height 6 × width 1 = 6 beats the previous best, so it becomes the running champion. The pair of bars 5,6 produces the winner (height 5 over width 2 = 10).

frames.caption: heights[top] = heights[2] = 5 ≥ cur = 2 → pop bar 2 and measure it.
frames.note: A shorter bar just arrived, so the bar on top can NEVER extend past it — this is the precise moment its maximal rectangle is finalized. The popped bar's height is the limiting height; the right boundary is the current index i (first shorter bar to the right) and the left boundary is the new stack top (first shorter bar to the left, or -1). Width = i - left - 1 spans every bar strictly between those two shorter walls. We score h*width against best.

frames.caption: left = 1 (bar below / -1), width = i - left - 1 = 4 - 1 - 1 = 2, area = 5×2 = 10.
frames.note: The subtle part: width is NOT i - poppedIndex. It is the full span strictly between the left boundary (the element now below on the stack, or -1 if empty) and i. That is why the formula reaches for the element BELOW the popped one — every bar between those two shorter walls was at least this tall, so the rectangle of this height stretches across all of them.

frames.caption: area 10 > best → best = 10. New largest rectangle: height 5 across width 2.
frames.note: best tracks the biggest finalized rectangle seen so far. Here height 5 × width 2 = 10 beats the previous best, so it becomes the running champion. The pair of bars 5,6 produces the winner (height 5 over width 2 = 10).

frames.caption: Push index 4 (height 2). The stack stays increasing; bar 4 now waits for its own shorter bar.
frames.note: The current bar is at least as tall as the stack top, so the increasing run continues — this bar might still extend rightward into taller territory, so its rectangle is not yet decided. We push its index and move on. The stack always holds a strictly increasing sequence of heights (by index), which is exactly the invariant that lets each pop know its left boundary instantly.

frames.caption: i = 5: cur = heights[5] = 3. Compare against the stack top before pushing.
frames.note: Each iteration first drains every stack bar that the current bar is too short to let continue (the while-loop), then pushes the current index. The current bar both ends taller bars’ rectangles and starts its own run.

frames.caption: Push index 5 (height 3). The stack stays increasing; bar 5 now waits for its own shorter bar.
frames.note: The current bar is at least as tall as the stack top, so the increasing run continues — this bar might still extend rightward into taller territory, so its rectangle is not yet decided. We push its index and move on. The stack always holds a strictly increasing sequence of heights (by index), which is exactly the invariant that lets each pop know its left boundary instantly.

frames.caption: i = n → cur = sentinel 0. A height-0 bar is shorter than all, forcing every remaining bar to pop.
frames.note: i has reached n, so cur is the sentinel height 0. Zero is shorter than every real bar, so it forces the while-loop to pop and measure every bar still on the stack — no leftover bar escapes being scored. This is the trick that avoids a separate flush loop after the scan.

frames.caption: heights[top] = heights[5] = 3 ≥ cur = 0 → pop bar 5 and measure it.
frames.note: A shorter bar just arrived, so the bar on top can NEVER extend past it — this is the precise moment its maximal rectangle is finalized. The popped bar's height is the limiting height; the right boundary is the current index i (first shorter bar to the right) and the left boundary is the new stack top (first shorter bar to the left, or -1). Width = i - left - 1 spans every bar strictly between those two shorter walls. We score h*width against best.

frames.caption: left = 4 (bar below / -1), width = i - left - 1 = 6 - 4 - 1 = 1, area = 3×1 = 3.
frames.note: The subtle part: width is NOT i - poppedIndex. It is the full span strictly between the left boundary (the element now below on the stack, or -1 if empty) and i. That is why the formula reaches for the element BELOW the popped one — every bar between those two shorter walls was at least this tall, so the rectangle of this height stretches across all of them.

frames.caption: area 3 ≤ best 10 → best stays 10.
frames.note: This bar’s maximal rectangle (3) does not beat the current best (10), so best is unchanged. We still HAD to measure it — every bar must be scored exactly once, at the moment a shorter bar pops it.

frames.caption: heights[top] = heights[4] = 2 ≥ cur = 0 → pop bar 4 and measure it.
frames.note: A shorter bar just arrived, so the bar on top can NEVER extend past it — this is the precise moment its maximal rectangle is finalized. The popped bar's height is the limiting height; the right boundary is the current index i (first shorter bar to the right) and the left boundary is the new stack top (first shorter bar to the left, or -1). Width = i - left - 1 spans every bar strictly between those two shorter walls. We score h*width against best.

frames.caption: left = 1 (bar below / -1), width = i - left - 1 = 6 - 1 - 1 = 4, area = 2×4 = 8.
frames.note: The subtle part: width is NOT i - poppedIndex. It is the full span strictly between the left boundary (the element now below on the stack, or -1 if empty) and i. That is why the formula reaches for the element BELOW the popped one — every bar between those two shorter walls was at least this tall, so the rectangle of this height stretches across all of them.

frames.caption: area 8 ≤ best 10 → best stays 10.
frames.note: This bar’s maximal rectangle (8) does not beat the current best (10), so best is unchanged. We still HAD to measure it — every bar must be scored exactly once, at the moment a shorter bar pops it.

frames.caption: heights[top] = heights[1] = 1 ≥ cur = 0 → pop bar 1 and measure it.
frames.note: A shorter bar just arrived, so the bar on top can NEVER extend past it — this is the precise moment its maximal rectangle is finalized. The popped bar's height is the limiting height; the right boundary is the current index i (first shorter bar to the right) and the left boundary is the new stack top (first shorter bar to the left, or -1). Width = i - left - 1 spans every bar strictly between those two shorter walls. We score h*width against best.

frames.caption: left = -1 (bar below / -1), width = i - left - 1 = 6 - -1 - 1 = 6, area = 1×6 = 6.
frames.note: The subtle part: width is NOT i - poppedIndex. It is the full span strictly between the left boundary (the element now below on the stack, or -1 if empty) and i. That is why the formula reaches for the element BELOW the popped one — every bar between those two shorter walls was at least this tall, so the rectangle of this height stretches across all of them.

frames.caption: area 6 ≤ best 10 → best stays 10.
frames.note: This bar’s maximal rectangle (6) does not beat the current best (10), so best is unchanged. We still HAD to measure it — every bar must be scored exactly once, at the moment a shorter bar pops it.

frames.caption: Scan complete — return best = 10. Largest rectangle is the 5,6 pair: height 5 × width 2.
frames.note: Every bar was measured exactly when a shorter bar popped it, and the sentinel flushed the rest. The winner is height 5 stretched over the two bars at indices 2 and 3 (the 5,6 pair) for area 10. Total work: each of the n indices is pushed once and popped once, so the nested while-loop is amortized O(n) overall, not O(n²).
